what_it_does: Acceptance test for the agent-session mailbox substrate: it exercises the FsStore mail leaf, cross-process append concurrency, and the MCP tools (sessions/session_post) over a temporary store.
observations: Uses an isolated tempfile store per section and never touches ~/company/.data.

Section A tests append-discipline refusals, monotonic unique seqs, thread defaulting, CAS body round-trips, since/to/verb/thread/limit reads, per-consumer cursors with regression refusal, corrupt-cursor failure, and reload survival.
Section B spawns 4 real subprocesses that each append 25 mails to test cross-process seq uniqueness under a graph_lock, with a 120-second hard timeout that exits 2 if a child hangs.

Section C registers the MCP tools via the pkgutil register(mcp, suite) contract, checks ToolAnnotations honesty (read-only vs write), seeds a NodeRegistry/Suite, and tests auto-routing by session state, explicit-verb refusals that teach, consult fan records, inbox round-trip and pagination, watch filtering, and describe joining registry and mail summaries.
Includes a structural floor assertion that the tools module never imports subprocess, ui_claude_session, or runtime.implement.
Exit codes are explicit: 0 PASS, 1 FAIL, 2 BLOCKED.
standouts: Cross-process append storm with hard timeout and BLOCKED exit code to catch lock deadlocks.
MCP ToolAnnotations honesty check (F10.1) distinguishing read-only sessions from write session_post.
Router auto-routing by registry state and explicit-verb refusals that carry teaching error messages.
Floor assertion separating intent-posting tools from supervisor spawn/runtime implementation imports.
conventions: Docstring maps test intent to labeled sections A/B/C matching the code structure.
Uses a small check() helper and expect_teaching() helper for ValueError needle matching.
Inserts project ROOT into sys.path and imports store/fs_store and mcp_face/tools/sessions.
Reads an internal FastMCP dev._tool_manager._tools to verify registration.
Respects an environment knob COMPANY_FABRIC_CONCURRENCY for consult copy cap testing.
concerns: expect_teaching catches a broad Exception before checking for ValueError, which could mask unexpected error types.
The missing-field append loop builds lambdas with a default argument bound to a reused dict reference; behavior depends on whether append_agent_mail reports all missing fields or only the first.
Section C pokes a private FastMCP attribute (dev._tool_manager._tools), making it fragile to SDK changes.
The 120-second subprocess timeout is long and a hung child is killed rather than diagnosed.
The test reads COMPANY_FABRIC_CONCURRENCY from the environment without setting it, so cap behavior depends on ambient configuration.
notes: A heavy acceptance test that treats the mailbox store and MCP face as a contract, proving durability, cross-process safety, and teaching error behavior.
questions: Where are FsStore.append_agent_mail, agent_mail_since, agent_mail_cursor, and set_agent_mail_cursor implemented?
What is the exact contract of NodeRegistry.discover and runtime.suite.Suite?
How does graph_lock achieve cross-process mutual exclusion for mail.jsonl appends?
What schema do agent_sessions.* event kinds follow for the watch operation?
Which supervisor service consumes the posted intents and how does it interpret deliver/wake/queue/consult verbs?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose in the docstring matches the three executed sections (store leaf, cross-process uniqueness, MCP face).
apparent_themes: Acceptance testing as living contract proof.
Teaching error messages over silent failure.
Separation between posting intents and acting on them.
intention_signals: The file is trying to demonstrate that the agent-session mailbox is a durable, concurrent-safe substrate with a well-behaved MCP surface that routes by session state and educates callers when they misuse verbs or constraints.
novelty: The cross-process append storm to validate file-lock teeth and the structural floor assertion that the MCP tool module must not import spawn/runtime implementation paths.
contribution: An acceptance harness that pins down mail-store semantics, cross-process seq uniqueness, and MCP tool behavior (registration, annotations, routing, refusals, round-trips) for the agent-session fabric.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test exercising the FsStore agent-mail leaf, cross-process append concurrency, and MCP sessions/session_post tools over isolated temporary stores.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the mailbox substrate and its MCP face satisfy durability, concurrency, routing, and teaching-error contracts.

symbols.does: Test assertion helper that prints ok/FAIL and accumulates failures.
symbols.description: Increments a global check counter, prints a pass or fail line, and appends the check name to FAILURES when the condition is false.

symbols.does: Helper asserting a callable raises ValueError whose message contains all required teaching needles.
symbols.description: Runs the supplied function, catches ValueError and verifies every needle appears in the message; other exceptions or no exception are reported as failures.

symbols.does: Tests the FsStore agent-mail leaf in isolation.
symbols.description: Validates append discipline refusals, monotonic seqs, thread defaults, CAS round-trips, filtered reads, cursor semantics, regression refusal, corruption handling, and reload survival on a temporary store.

symbols.does: Cross-process stress test for unique mail seqs.
symbols.description: Spawns four subprocesses that each append 25 mails, waits with a hard timeout, then verifies 100 parseable rows with all seqs unique across processes.

symbols.does: Tests the MCP sessions/session_post tools end-to-end.
symbols.description: Registers tools on a dev FastMCP, checks annotations, seeds registry state, exercises auto-routing, explicit-verb refusals, consult fan, inbox pagination, watch filtering, and describe joining.

symbols.does: Entry point running sections A, B, and C on separate temp stores and reporting exit status.
symbols.description: Creates a temporary directory per section, invokes it, then prints check/failure counts and exits 0, 1, or 2 based on results.
